competitors.label: MDN Event bubbling
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the closest canonical docs page because it covers propagation and event delegation in the same learning path.
theirStrengths: It explains delegation through propagation mechanics and anchors the idea in standard DOM behavior.
theirGaps: Delegation is part of a broader propagation lesson rather than the sole focus of the page.

realWorldUseCases: The production/debug value is not just fewer listeners. It is surviving dynamic DOM updates, nested click targets, and propagation edge cases without silently handling the wrong element.
followUpConfusion: Not every event is an ideal delegation candidate. focus/blur behave differently from click.

realWorldUseCases: MDN shows delegation as practical DOM behavior, but our page is now more directly anchored to app-style dynamic-list bugs.
actionableExamples: Its examples remain concrete enough that the actionable comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: MDN explains propagation well, while our page now answers nested-target, stopPropagation, and non-bubbling-event follow-ups more directly.

actionableExamples: Add one before/after snippet that contrasts per-item listeners with delegated handling on the same markup.

competitors.winReason: Our page now focuses more directly on dynamic DOM, nested click targets, and propagation-edge-case debugging, while MDN teaches delegation inside a broader propagation lesson.

competitors.label: javascript.info Event delegation
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info has one of the strongest tutorial pages dedicated specifically to event delegation.
theirStrengths: It is focused specifically on delegation and includes concrete DOM examples that developers can copy.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly framed around frontend-debug edge cases than our page.

realWorldUseCases: The production/debug value is not just fewer listeners. It is surviving dynamic DOM updates, nested click targets, and propagation edge cases without silently handling the wrong element.
followUpConfusion: Delegation depends on propagation; if a child calls stopPropagation(), the parent listener may never run.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info already uses strong menu and table scenarios, but our page now surfaces more explicit modern UI bug patterns.
actionableExamples: Its worked examples are still detailed enough that the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page now says more directly when delegation breaks because of nested targets, stopPropagation, or non-bubbling events.

actionableExamples: Add one complete table/menu example where rows are inserted after listener registration to widen the actionable gap.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info remains a strong delegation tutorial, but our page is now stronger on nested-target bugs, stopPropagation edge cases, and accessibility-adjacent follow-ups.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Event delegation
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep question about event delegation.
competitors.winReason: Our page now pairs the concept with a nested-target closest() example and explicit propagation caveats, while GreatFrontEnd stays more definition-first.
theirStrengths: It is tightly aligned with interview phrasing and stays focused on the delegation concept.
theirGaps: It is lighter on nested-target and propagation-edge-case debugging than our shipped trivia.

realWorldUseCases: The production/debug value is not just fewer listeners. It is surviving dynamic DOM updates, nested click targets, and propagation edge cases without silently handling the wrong element.
GreatFrontEnd explains why delegation helps, but it is lighter on a concrete nested-target production bug.
actionableExamples: Its answer is concise, while our page now includes both a basic delegated list and a closest()-guarded production example.
followUpConfusion: The overall follow-up comparison is still close because GreatFrontEnd stays tightly aligned with the interview concept.
Add one short note on pointerenter or mouseenter-style events so the non-bubbling follow-up set is broader.
